titleStmt.title: An Cnoc Buí | Knockboy 1

editor.orgName: Maynooth University, Department of Early Irish
Maynooth University, Department of Early Irish

titleStmt.funder: Irish Research Council (grant no. IRC/W001985/1) and Arts and Humanities Research Council (grant no. AH/W001985/1)

funder.ref: UK-Ireland Collaboration in the Digital Humanities Research Grant

respStmt.name: Simon Dowling
respStmt.resp: 3D photogrammetry and processed using Agisoft Photoscan software. Re-processed and uploaded to sketchfab in 2020.

msItem.textLang: Primitive Irish written in ogham script

support.p: National Monuments Service SMR ID: WA013-034006-

supportDesc.condition: The beginning of the inscription is lost, nothing remains before the present initial letter. The B surface of the inscription has been chipped away to such an extent that the letters that Macalister (1945, 286) supplied, and the B-half of the letter R, have disappeared. The lintel and surrounding wall underwent urgent stabilisation work by Tom Pollard and his team in 2019 as part of the Knockboy Conservation Project, thanks to funding and support from the Heritage Office at Waterford County Council, the Heritage Council, the National Monuments Service and the Adopt a Monument Scheme.

layoutDesc.layout: The inscription is on two angles of the stone, unusually (though not uniquely) reading up on the right angle and down the left angle. The inscription begins on the face turned toward the inside of the church in its current position and continues over the top to the right.

history.provenance: Discovered in 1851 by G. V. du Noyer. Found acting as an inner lintel of the north window, next to the east gable, of the ruin of Seskinan parish church (Macalister 1945, 286). The current church (WA013-034001-) was probably built on the site of an earlier church. There were traces of an ecclesiastical enclosure in the field to the south and within the graveyard (Brash 1868-9, 127; Power 1898, 84), but this is no longer evident. There is also the site of Toberatemple Well (WA013-032001-) c. 250m to the west (Power 1898, 83). The church is an undifferentiated structure (int. dims. 22.2m E-W; 7.55m N-S) surviving complete (H of long walls c. 2.1-2.5m) with quoins and a double bellcote (now damaged) over the west gable. There are pointed doorways towards the west end of the north and south walls, with a stoup inside the south doorway and an aumbry at the east end of the south wall. Two ogee-headed windows, one above the other, are in the west gable, with another in the east wall, and there are two simple lights towards the east end of the long walls. All the embrasures are lintelled internally except the east window which has a flat arch of dressed voussoirs and ingoings. The square base of a font (WA013-034004-) is also located within the church (Power 1898, 83). All of the lintels, except one over the north doorway, have ogham inscriptions (Brash 1868-9, 118-30; Macalister 1945, 286-9), and a seventh ogham stone is standing in the north-west corner of the church. An eighth ogham stone, said to have also originated here, was recorded by Macalister (1907, 213; 1945, 289) at Salterbridge House, near Cappoquin prior to 1907 but it was missing when he returned in 1940. A ninth fragment of an ogham stone was discovered in the fabric of the church by stone mason Tom Pollard during conservation work in 2021.

Still acting as lintel over the north window of the church at Knockboy. The church and ogham stones were recorded in 3d by Simon Dowling as part of the Knockboy Conservation Project in 2016 (funded by the Heritage Council of Ireland and supported by Waterford County Council).

listChange.change: Jef Bucas autogenerated EpiDoc output from spreadsheet
Added and encoded details, including edition.
Added information and images from O3D.
Added ab type list to edition.
Updated layout, handnote, edition and apparatus
Added 3d recording details and resp, site description and Dave's plan

app.note: Presumably some letters are missing from the beginning of the inscription, but there is no trace of any strokes on the H-aicme immediately preceeding the ER[.]T. The B side and the arris itself are more battered, so perhsps some B-aicme consonanats and/or vowels have been lost. Between the R and the T, there is space enough for 2 or 3 vowel notches. Although there is space for a final I, no trace survives. The formula word MUCOI, though badly damaged, can be fairly confidently reconstructed. There is also damage to the original top of the stone, where the inscription continues over and down the other side, resulting in the loss of some letters. Macalister's suggested EGAM appears to fit and completes a name attested in two other west Waterford inscriptions (see commentary below).

div.p: The kin-group name ia almost certainly MUCOI NETASEGAMONAS ‘the kin of Nad-Segamon (‘champion of *Segamū')'. One theory is that this may be the Gaulish name Segomo (McManus 1991, 179 n. 46). It is noteworthy that a Nia-Segamon is listed in the historical sources as a prehistoric king of Cashel (Bhreathnach 2014, 43-4). The fact that MUCOI NETA-SEGAMONAS '(of) the descendant of Nad/Nia-Segamon' is also found on an ogham stone at nearby Ardmore I (I-WAT-002) and at Island (I-WAT-042) may suggest that this was a dynasty which controlled the area of west Waterford in the 5th century.
